replies.msg:
Yes. a few times.

I went to a meeting with my staff. We are tax auditor and I was the file manager.
As usual I dress like I just came back from fishing trip.
Taxpayer is a world leader News agency. They have 4 lawyers, 2 external chartered accountants , their VP of Finance and their tax manager.
We were there to discuss my staff's proposed tax adjustments. It was about $2 million additional taxes. My staff was a white Canadian in his 50s and he wore a 3 piece suit. I had another meeting in the same area that morning and I was about 5 minutes late. My staff told them "Hi this is XX from my office". He forgot to introduce me as his boss.
Everybody from the opposite side ignored me during the whole meeting.
I was interrupted three times by their lawyers when I want to ask some questions.

Taxpayer asked my staff did their presentations changed his mind about the assessments. My staff then turned to me and asked what was my decision. I can see the embarrassed reactions on the lawyer's face.
I told my staff... No change to our proposed assessment and walked out the room.

My staff later told me that the lawyers thought I was his assistant that was why they didn't address my questions.

Have some other stories but don't want to bore you.

replies.msg:
漢族 is originally a cultural classification, not ethnical. when the Mongolian Empire ruled China (definitely not 元朝, 元 is a division of Mongolian Empire, not a dynasty of China), people were classified in 蒙古人、色目人、漢人、南人. People of 南宋 were 南人 not 漢人.

中國 was a geographic concept. Not until Qing does it come to referring the country. And 中文 meaned all 3 of 滿文、蒙文、漢文(文言文) in Treaty of Nerchinsk of 1689, the first document
in international law that China is treated as a sovereign state, which is about the border between Qing and Russia. 中文 (the 3 languages) were used on the border markers.

The concept of 中國人, 中華民族("Chinese nation") and "Chinese ethnicity" is created in late Qing, when the idea of a nation-state of China should fight for independence from Qing (驅逐達虜恢復中華) completed with the idea to work out a multinational state (漢滿蒙回藏五族共和). Some even argued that 中國 (China proper 本土/漢地十八省) was a colony of Qing. But the "internal" differences of "Chinese ethnicity" were neglected.

Although HK nation is being raised is based on civic nationalism against Chinese ethnonationalism, that means anyone of "Chinese ethnicity" may be accepted as a member of the HK nation, I would rather identify us, or at least myself, as Cantonese ethnicity. (Actually I am also of Teochew ethnicity. )

Look, I can not stop what others think.
People who came from Mainland China, Republic of China, Hong Kong, Macau or from Singapore etc are being labelled by the mainstream here as Chinese Canadian or Canadian Chinese.

A few times I was asked if I am Chinese, I would tell them I was born in Hong Kong and we are VERY different from people who came from China. Many Canadian (including Caucasian,South Asian, Oriental) can tell the difference as after they interact with us... e.g. common courtesy, good manners etc.
I always considered myself as Canadian.
I immigrated to Canada and proud to be a Canadian...

About 20 years ago, i drove my family up to a Provincial Park to see the "Fall Colours" (red Maple leaves). There was a baby black bear eating wild berries on the side of the highway. So I stopped and took some pictures from my car. There were about 4 cars slowly stopped behind me and took picture from their car.
Then, all of a sudden, a car driven by "Chinese" abruptly stopped and parked in front of my car (blocking my view). That also scared the baby bear and the bear began to move away. Soon after that car parked, a very excited Chinese girl jumped out and chased the baby bear.
Apparently, she wanted to touch the baby bear. All the drivers and some of the passengers from the cars that parked behind me started to yell at her to stop it. When that Chinese (mandarin speaking) girl returned to her car, one of the driver walked up to that car and told her the Mama Bear was hiding about 20 meters away from the baby bear. They saw the Mama bear was about to chase her. They yelled out loud to scare her away.
That driver walked towards my car and asked me if i am also Chinese... I told 'hell No', I am a Canadian born in Hong Kong.. We have manners..

The story doesn't end there.. Something happened later in the same Park that I have to repeat that I am not Chinese....

Look, I can not stop what others think.
People who came from Mainland China, Republic of China, Hong Kong, Macau or from Singapore etc are being labelled by the mainstream here as Chinese Canadian or Canadian Chinese.

A few times I was asked if I am Chinese, I would tell them I was born in Hong Kong and we are VERY different from people who came from China. Many Canadian (including Caucasian,South Asian, Oriental) can tell the difference as after they interact with us... e.g. common courtesy, good manners etc.
I always considered myself as Canadian.
I immigrated to Canada and proud to be a Canadian...

About 20 years ago, i drove my family up to a Provincial Park to see the "Fall Colours" (red Maple leaves). There was a baby black bear eating wild berries on the side of the highway. So I stopped and took some pictures from my car. There were about 4 cars slowly stopped behind me and took picture from their car.
Then, all of a sudden, a car driven by "Chinese" abruptly stopped and parked in front of my car (blocking my view). That also scared the baby bear and the bear began to move away. Soon after that car parked, a very excited Chinese girl jumped out and chased the baby bear.
Apparently, she wanted to touch the baby bear. All the drivers and some of the passengers from the cars that parked behind me started to yell at her to stop it. When that Chinese (mandarin speaking) girl returned to her car, one of the driver walked up to that car and told her the Mama Bear was hiding about 20 meters away from the baby bear. They saw the Mama bear was about to chase her. They yelled out loud to scare her away.
That driver walked towards my car and asked me if i am also Chinese... I told 'hell No', I am a Canadian born in Hong Kong.. We have manners..

The story doesn't end there.. Something happened later in the same Park that I have to repeat that I am not Chinese....
\standing less than 20 menot to do that again.

Continue with the Park story..
This incident happened before we saw the baby bear.
There was a small museum inside the Park. We got there early and the museum is still closed. The parking lot has less than 10 cars waiting. There were heavy rain the night before and the ground was very clean....
We waited in our car on a spot near the entrance.
I was dowsing off after a 4 hour drive. I was awaken by a van screeching to a stop at the front entrance.. . Seven Chinese men and women came out from the van and each and every one of them all spitted on the very beautiful and clean" side walk. Some of them started lighting up a cigarette, started smoking and talking very loud. They had disrupted me and other who were enjoying the morning light breeze near a lake and birds singing...

When the door opened up, one of the driver walked pass my car and asked (pointing to the van with the seven Chinese) if we were together. I told him NO, I was born in Hong Kong and they were from Mainland China. I am different from them......
